# SKI HERE — Utah Public Ski Area Pilot Project

> SKI HERE is a proposal for a Utah state-sponsored public ski area with resident-first pricing — $59 adult day tickets, $750 season passes, and a target opening of 2029. The pilot is modeled on New York's Olympic Regional Development Authority (ORDA) and applies the same resident-first principle Utah already uses for public golf courses and tiered hunting permits.

The site presents the case for the project, the proposed structure, financial plan, governance options, and call to action.

## Core facts

- **Location:** Northern Wasatch Range, ~30–35 miles from Salt Lake City. Base elevation ~7,200 ft, summit ~9,700 ft. On Wasatch-Cache National Forest land under a USFS Special Use Permit.
- **Target opening:** 2029 (Phase 1: 3 lifts + magic carpet, ~500 acres, full base lodge)
- **Resident pricing:** $59 adult day ticket, $30 child (under 12), $750 season pass, $15/student school programs
- **Non-resident pricing:** $119 day ticket, $1,250 season pass
- **Total capital:** ~$73.6M across all phases. ~$64.3M (87%) identified across 8 funding sources.
- **Year 1 revenue projection:** $5.7M against $4.78M operating costs (~$940K surplus)
- **Stabilized capacity:** 180,000 annual skier visits (Year 6+)

## Why now

- Utah lift ticket prices up ~240% since 2000 vs. CPI ~87%
- Population doubled (1.7M → 3.5M, 1990–2025) with essentially no new resort capacity
- Per-capita resort access has fallen 48%
- Average Utah skier age is now 48 — without intervention, the 2034 Olympics will showcase world-class skiing to a Utah audience that can no longer afford to participate

## Pages

- [Home / Proposal](https://skihere.org/) — Problem, proposal, pricing, precedent, Olympic context, next steps, get-involved
- [Financial Plan](https://skihere.org/financials) — Capital costs, operating costs, revenue projections, funding sources, risk analysis, governance models
- [Full content for AI ingestion](https://skihere.org/llms-full.txt) — Markdown version of all on-site content
